lesson_title: Compare Fractions with the Same Numerator
objective: Compare two fractions with the same numerator by reasoning about the size of the parts

pretest.question: Ava has 2/4 of a ribbon. Zoe has 2/6 of the same ribbon. Who has more?

learn.instruction: When two fractions have the SAME NUMERATOR, you have the same number of pieces. But the pieces are different sizes! A smaller denominator means each piece is BIGGER. Think: splitting a pizza into 3 slices → big slices. Splitting into 8 slices → small slices. Example: 2/3 vs 2/8 → 2 big slices vs 2 small slices → 2/3 > 2/8.
learn.question: Which is greater: 1/2 or 1/6?
learn.explanation: Same numerator (1). Halves are bigger than sixths → 1/2 > 1/6.

learn.instruction: Use fraction strips to see this. Two bars, same length. Bar A split into 4 parts → each part is big. Bar B split into 8 parts → each part is small. If you shade 3 parts on each: 3/4 is clearly more area than 3/8.
learn.question: Look at two fraction strips. Both have 2 parts shaded. One is split into 3 parts, the other into 6 parts. Which shows the greater fraction?

learn.instruction: Remember: SAME numerator → look at denominators. SMALLER denominator = BIGGER pieces = GREATER fraction. This is the opposite of what you might expect!
learn.question: Compare: 4/8 ○ 4/4
learn.explanation: Same numerator (4). Fourths are bigger pieces than eighths → 4/4 > 4/8.

try.question: Compare: 3/6 ○ 3/4
try.hint: Same numerator (3). Which denominator gives bigger pieces?
guided_steps: Step 3: Fourths are bigger than sixths.

try.question: Two same-size water bottles. Liam drank 2/3 of his. Nora drank 2/8 of hers. Who drank more water?
try.hint: Same numerator (2). Compare the size of the pieces.
guided_steps: Step 3: Thirds are bigger than eighths.

practice_r1.question: Two same-size ropes. One has 4/8 cut off. The other has 4/3 cut off. Which had more cut off?

practice_r2.question: Hana walked 3/4 of a trail. Jin walked 3/6 of the same trail. Who walked farther? Write a comparison.
Write a fraction with numerator 3 that is GREATER than 3/8.

practice_r3.question: Yuna says 1/8 > 1/3 because 8 > 3. What would you tell Yuna?
practice_r3.answer: The denominator tells the size of each piece, not how much you have. Bigger denominator = smaller pieces. Since eighths are smaller than thirds, 1/8 < 1/3.

practice_r3.question: Put these fractions in order from GREATEST to LEAST: 2/8, 2/3, 2/6, 2/4
Without drawing a picture, explain why 4/6 > 4/8.
practice_r3.answer: Same numerator (4). Sixths are bigger pieces than eighths because the whole is split into fewer parts. 4 bigger pieces > 4 smaller pieces, so 4/6 > 4/8.
